title: Binary Search Tree (BST) Structure
subject: Data Structures (DS)
textbook_reference: Seymour Lipschutz, Data Structures, McGraw Hill, Chapter 7, Page 215

recent_questions: Create a Binary Search Tree for the following data elements: 50, 25, 75, 12, 30, 60, 85, 5. Write inorder, preorder, and postorder traversals (Dec 2023).
Explain BST deletion cases with a suitable example (May 2023).

theory: A Binary Search Tree (BST) is a node-based binary tree data structure where each node has at most two children. It satisfies the BST property: for any given node, the key value of all nodes in its left subtree is strictly less than the node's key, and the key value of all nodes in its right subtree is strictly greater than the node's key. This property makes searching, insertion, and deletion highly efficient, operating in O(log n) average time complexity. Tree traversal algorithms include Inorder (Left, Root, Right - which outputs elements in sorted ascending order), Preorder (Root, Left, Right), and Postorder (Left, Right, Root).
key_points: Left Subtree < Root < Right Subtree for all nodes in the tree.
Inorder traversal of a BST always yields sorted data in ascending order.
Search/Insert/Delete average time complexity is O(log n); worst case is O(n) for a skewed tree.
Deletion involves 3 cases: deleting a leaf node, deleting a node with one child, and deleting a node with two children (replaced by its inorder successor or predecessor).
Balanced variants like AVL and Red-Black trees prevent worst-case O(n) behavior.
use_cases: Implementing database indexing systems.
Implementing efficient associative arrays and symbol tables in compilers.

title: Hasse Diagram of Poset
subject: Discrete Structures & Graph Theory (DSGT)
textbook_reference: Kenneth H. Rosen, Discrete Mathematics and its Applications, Chapter 9, Page 620

recent_questions: Draw the Hasse diagram for the divisibility relation on the set A = {2, 3, 6, 12, 24, 36} (May 2024).
Define Poset. Draw Hasse diagram for subset relation on power set of {a, b, c} (Dec 2023).

theory: A Hasse diagram is a graphical representation of a finite partially ordered set (Poset). It simplifies the poset graph by eliminating redundant information based on the mathematical properties of partial orders: reflexivity and transitivity. In a Hasse diagram, elements are represented as vertices in a plane, and directed edges are drawn upwards between elements x and y if x is related to y (x < y) and there is no element z such that x < z < y (y covers x). Loop arrows for reflexivity and transitive edges are omitted. If two related elements are connected, the larger element is placed vertically higher.
key_points: Represents partial orders (relations that are reflexive, antisymmetric, and transitive).
All loops representing reflexivity (x relates to x) are omitted.
All transitive connections (if x<y and y<z, then x<z) are omitted.
Edges are drawn upward to indicate order; arrowheads are unnecessary.
Vertices at the bottom are minimal/minimum; vertices at the top are maximal/maximum.
use_cases: Representing task dependency hierarchies in project scheduling.
Modeling security privilege level access lattices.

title: T Flip-Flop from JK Flip-Flop Conversion
subject: Digital Logic & Computer Architecture (DLCA)
textbook_reference: M. Morris Mano, Digital Design, Pearson, Chapter 6, Page 221

recent_questions: Convert JK flip-flop to T flip-flop and verify the conversion with truth table (Dec 2023).
Explain the working of T Flip-Flop. How is it constructed from a JK Flip-Flop? (May 2024).

theory: A T (Toggle) Flip-Flop is a synchronous sequential circuit element that changes its output state (toggles) on each clock edge if its control input T is high (T = 1). If T is low (T = 0), the flip-flop maintains its current state. T Flip-Flops are commonly implemented by converting existing JK Flip-Flops. The conversion is achieved by tying the J and K inputs of the JK Flip-Flop together to a single terminal labeled T. The characteristic equation of the JK flip-flop is Q(next) = J·Q̄ + K̄·Q; substituting J = K = T yields the characteristic equation of the T flip-flop: Q(next) = T·Q̄ + T̄·Q.
key_points: Formed by connecting the J and K inputs of a JK flip-flop to a common terminal T.
Characteristic equation: Q(next) = T·Q̄ + T̄·Q.
When T = 0, the flip-flop holds its previous state (Q(next) = Q).
When T = 1, the flip-flop toggles its output on each active clock edge (Q(next) = Q̄).
Simplest of all flip-flop conversions, requiring no logic gates.
use_cases: Binary counters and ripple counters where state toggling is required.
Frequency division circuits to divide clock frequency by two.

blocks.label: Equations: J = K = T
sublabels: Q(next) = T·Q̄ + T̄·Q  (Hold if T = 0, Toggle if T = 1)

title: 4-Bit Serial-In Serial-Out (SISO) Shift Register
subject: Digital Logic & Computer Architecture (DLCA)
textbook_reference: M. Morris Mano, Digital Design, Pearson, Chapter 6 (Registers and Counters), Page 255

recent_questions: Explain the working of 4-bit Serial-In Serial-Out (SISO) shift register with a neat diagram and timing waveform (Dec 2023).
Draw the block diagram of a 4-bit shift register using D flip-flops and show serial transfer of data (May 2024).

theory: A Shift Register is a synchronous sequential circuit designed to store and shift binary data. It is composed of flip-flops connected in series, where the output of each flip-flop is linked to the input of the next stage. A common clock signal is supplied to all flip-flops to synchronize data movement. In a 4-Bit Serial-In Serial-Out (SISO) Shift Register, four D Flip-Flops are cascaded. Serial input data is fed into the first flip-flop (FF0). With each clock pulse, the data bit moves one step to the right through the register until it emerges from the output of the final stage (FF3) as serial output. For an N-bit shift register, N clock cycles are needed to load N bits of data serially, and N-1 additional cycles are needed to read the data completely.
key_points: Formed by cascading four D flip-flops in series (FF0 to FF3).
Uses a common clock input for synchronous shifting of all bits.
Serial Input (SI) connects to the input of the first stage, Serial Output (SO) is taken from the last stage.
Data transfers by one position to the right with each active clock edge.
Requires N clock cycles to load N bits, and another N-1 clock cycles to fully shift out the data.
Serves as the basis for temporary data storage, digital delays, and serial communication.
use_cases: Temporal delay generation in digital communication lines.
Serial communication links and data transmitters/receivers.
First-In, First-Out (FIFO) buffers in microprocessor systems.

title: 4-Bit Ring Counter (Circulating Register)
subject: Digital Logic & Computer Architecture (DLCA)
textbook_reference: M. Morris Mano, Digital Design, Pearson, Chapter 6, Page 262

recent_questions: Explain the working of a 4-bit Ring Counter using D flip-flops. Draw its timing diagram (Dec 2023).
What is a Ring Counter? How does it differ from a Ripple Counter? Draw a 4-bit ring counter circuit (May 2023).

theory: A Ring Counter is a specialized shift register with a feedback path from the output of the last stage (FF3) back to the input of the first stage (FF0). It circulates a single '1' (or a pattern) continuously around the ring of flip-flops. For a 4-bit ring counter, only 4 of the 16 possible states are used (0001, 0010, 0100, 1000). It requires an initial 'preset' or 'load' operation to inject the circulating '1'. Unlike a ripple (asynchronous) counter, a ring counter is a synchronous sequential circuit — all flip-flops share a common clock.
key_points: Output of the last flip-flop (FF3.Q) is fed back to the input of the first flip-flop (FF0.D).
For a 4-bit ring counter, the sequence is: 1000 → 0100 → 0010 → 0001 → 1000...
Only N states are used for an N-bit ring counter (not 2^N), making it self-decoding — no extra decoding logic is needed.
Requires initial loading of a '1' into one stage via preset/clear; all other stages must be '0'.
Uses N flip-flops to count N states, compared to ⌈log₂N⌉ flip-flops in a binary counter.
use_cases: Generating non-overlapping clock phases for sequencing operations in control units.
State sequencers in synchronous finite state machines.
Round-robin scheduling hardware in digital control systems.

title: Three-Schema Database Architecture
subject: Database Management Systems (DBMS)
textbook_reference: Korth, Silberschatz & Sudarshan, Database System Concepts, Chapter 1, Page 24

recent_questions: Explain Three-Schema Architecture of a database system with a neat diagram. Describe data independence (Dec 2023).
What is DBMS? Explain the three levels of data abstraction (May 2023).

theory: The Three-Schema Architecture (also called the ANSI/SPARC architecture) is a framework used to describe the structure of a database system. It separates the user applications from the physical database by defining three distinct levels of schemas: (1) External Schema (View Level) which describes how individual users view the database; (2) Conceptual Schema (Logical Level) which describes what data is stored in the database and the relationships among the data; and (3) Internal Schema (Physical Level) which describes the physical storage structure of the database. This separation guarantees data independence, meaning modifications at one level do not force changes at other levels.
key_points: View Level (External): Multiple customized schemas for different users.
Logical Level (Conceptual): Single schema describing tables, fields, types, and constraints.
Physical Level (Internal): Single schema detailing file organizations, index paths, and storage blocks.
Logical Data Independence: Ability to modify conceptual schema without changing external schemas.
Physical Data Independence: Ability to modify physical schema without changing conceptual schema.
use_cases: Designing corporate enterprise databases supporting diverse departments.
Optimizing database disk access configurations without breaking existing SQL applications.

layers.name: Internal Level (Physical Schema)
layers.protocols: Data Files, Indexes, B-Trees, Record Layouts, Disk Blocks

title: Process Life Cycle (5-State Model)
subject: Operating Systems (OS)
textbook_reference: Abraham Silberschatz & Peter Galvin, Operating System Concepts, Chapter 3, Page 108

recent_questions: Draw and explain the 5-state process transition diagram in Operating Systems (May 2024).
Describe process lifecycle transitions. Explain what happens during context switching (Dec 2023).

theory: As a process executes, it changes state. The process lifecycle is modeled using transition states representing the current activity of the process. In the standard 5-state model, these states are: (1) New: The process is being created; (2) Ready: The process is in memory waiting to be assigned to a processor; (3) Running: Instructions are being executed by the CPU; (4) Waiting (Blocked): The process is waiting for some event to occur (such as an I/O completion or reception of a signal); and (5) Terminated: The process has finished execution. Transitions occur based on OS scheduler actions, hardware interrupts, or system calls.
key_points: New ➔ Ready: Admitted by the long-term scheduler.
Ready ➔ Running: Dispatched by the short-term CPU scheduler.
Running ➔ Ready: Triggered by timer interrupts or time quantum expiration.
Running ➔ Waiting: Triggered by I/O requests or block requests.
Waiting ➔ Ready: Triggered by I/O or event completion.
use_cases: Analyzing CPU scheduling policies (Round Robin, FCFS, Priority).
Debugging program deadlock situations where processes stay in the Blocked/Waiting state.

title: 7-State Process Transition Model
subject: Operating Systems (OS)
textbook_reference: William Stallings, Operating Systems: Internals and Design Principles, Chapter 3, Page 118

recent_questions: Draw and explain the 7-state process transition model in Operating Systems with a neat diagram. Highlight why suspended states are necessary (Dec 2023).
Describe process swapping. Explain the transition from Blocked/Suspend to Ready/Suspend (May 2023).

theory: The 7-state process transition model extends the standard 5-state model by introducing two suspended states to manage main memory (RAM) limitations in multitasking operating systems. When physical memory is saturated and all active processes are waiting for I/O, the CPU remains idle. To solve this, the Medium-Term Scheduler (MTS) swaps blocked processes out of RAM to secondary storage (disk), freeing memory for other processes. The two suspended states are: (1) Blocked/Suspend: The process is in secondary memory and is waiting for an I/O event to occur; and (2) Ready/Suspend: The process is in secondary memory but is ready to run as soon as it is swapped back into main memory.
key_points: Ready/Suspend: Ready process swapped out of RAM to disk to free space.
Blocked/Suspend: Blocked process swapped out of RAM to disk.
Medium-Term Scheduler (MTS): Manages process swapping and state transitions between memory and disk.
I/O Completion on Disk: Transports Blocked/Suspend process to Ready/Suspend state directly in secondary storage.
Memory Optimization: Prevents trashing and ensures CPU utilization is maximized during high-concurrency tasks.
use_cases: Swapping mechanism implementation in UNIX/Linux systems during high memory usage.
Designing virtual memory schedulers to manage process images between RAM and swap partition.

title: Paging Hardware Memory Mapping
subject: Operating Systems (OS)
textbook_reference: Abraham Silberschatz, Peter B. Galvin & Greg Gagne, Operating System Concepts, 9th Edition, Chapter 8, Page 356

recent_questions: Explain paging hardware with a neat memory mapping diagram. (Dec 2023)
What is paging? Explain paging hardware with translation mechanism. (May 2024)

theory: Paging is a memory management scheme that eliminates the need for contiguous allocation of physical memory. The process's logical address space can be non-contiguous, and the physical memory is divided into fixed-size blocks called frames. The logical memory is divided into blocks of the same size called pages. Every address generated by the CPU is divided into two parts: Page Number (p) and Page Offset (d). The page number (p) is used as an index into a page table. The page table contains the base address of each page in physical memory (the frame number f). The frame number (f) is combined with the page offset (d) to form the Physical Address (f, d), which points to the exact byte in the Physical Memory.
key_points: Logical Address is divided into Page Number (p) and Page Offset (d).
Page Table maps the Page Number (p) to the Physical Frame Number (f).
Page Offset (d) is copied directly to the Physical Address without any translation.
Physical Address consists of the Frame Number (f) and Page Offset (d).
Paging avoids external fragmentation and allows sharing of common code pages.
use_cases: Modern virtual memory systems implementing paging in Windows, Linux, and macOS.
Hardware-level address translation handled by the Memory Management Unit (MMU).
Enabling processes to run even when their virtual memory space is larger than physical RAM.

title: Segmentation Memory Mapping Hardware
subject: Operating Systems (OS)
textbook_reference: Abraham Silberschatz, Peter B. Galvin & Greg Gagne, Operating System Concepts, 10th Edition, Chapter 9 (Main Memory)

recent_questions: Explain the concept of segmentation. Describe with a neat diagram the hardware support for segmentation.
Draw and explain the address translation mechanism in a segmented memory system.

theory:
Segmentation is a memory-management scheme that supports a user-programmer's view of memory. Instead of viewing memory as a linear array of bytes (as in paging), memory is divided into variable-sized logical units called segments (e.g., code, stack, heap, data, library functions).

To map these logical segments into physical memory, hardware support is required in the form of a Segment Table. Each entry in the Segment Table corresponds to a segment and contains two primary values:
1. Base Address: The starting physical address in memory where the segment resides.
2. Limit: The exact length or size of that segment.

Address Translation Process:
1. The CPU generates a logical address containing a segment number (s) and an offset (d).
2. The segment number (s) is used to index into the Segment Table.
3. The hardware retrieves the Limit and Base for segment (s).
4. The offset (d) is compared against the Limit: d < Limit.
5. If the offset is invalid (d >= Limit), the hardware generates an interrupt/trap (Segmentation Fault / Access Violation).
6. If valid, the physical address is calculated as: Physical Address = Base + d.
7. The physical address is then used to access the appropriate location in physical memory.
key_points: Divides logical memory into variable-length segments reflecting the programmer's structure (Code, Data, Stack).
Logical Address consists of: Segment number (s) and Offset (d).
Segment Table entries have two parts: Limit (segment size) and Base (physical starting address).
Hardware protection: Comparator verifies that offset d is strictly less than Limit (d < Limit).
Invalid access triggers an addressing error trap (segmentation fault / core dump).
Valid access translates: Physical Address = Base + d.
use_cases: Process memory segmentation: Dividing a running process into text, data, heap, and stack segments.
Memory Protection: Tagging segments (e.g., Code as read-only and executable, Stack as non-executable).
Shared Memory: Allowing multiple processes to share common code libraries or shared memory segments for IPC.

title: TLB Hardware Paging Cache Memory Mapping
subject: Operating Systems (OS)
textbook_reference: Abraham Silberschatz, Peter B. Galvin & Greg Gagne, Operating System Concepts, Chapter 8 (Main Memory), Page 360

recent_questions: What is Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB)? Explain the hardware support for paging with a neat block diagram (Dec 2023).
Describe the paging scheme with TLB. Explain hit ratio and effective access time calculations (May 2024).

theory: A Translation Lookaside Buffer (TLB) is a high-speed associative hardware cache memory helper used to reduce the memory access latency of paging. When the CPU generates a logical address containing a Page Number (p) and an Offset (d), it must convert the Page Number (p) to a physical Frame Number (f). In simple paging, this requires searching the Page Table in main memory, costing an extra memory cycle. To speed this up, the TLB stores a small subset of recently used page-table entries (typically 64 to 1024 entries). The Page Number (p) is looked up in the TLB in parallel (associative search). On a TLB Hit, the Frame Number (f) is retrieved instantly. On a TLB Miss, the CPU must access the Page Table in main memory, retrieve the Frame Number, and update the TLB with the new page-frame pair for subsequent accesses.
key_points: TLB is an associative, high-speed cache memory implemented in hardware.
Parallel Search: TLB lookup of Page Number (p) takes less than 1 nanosecond.
TLB Hit: Retrieves Frame Number (f) immediately, avoiding a Page Table memory access.
TLB Miss: Retrieves Frame Number from the Page Table in memory, causing a latency penalty.
Effective Access Time (EAT) = (Hit Ratio * Hit Time) + (Miss Ratio * Miss Time).
TLB entries are flushed or tagged with an Address Space Identifier (ASID) during a process context switch.
use_cases: Hardware-based address translation acceleration in modern MMUs (Memory Management Units).
Evaluating CPU architecture performance using TLB hit/miss profiling tools.

title: Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) — Deadlock State
subject: Operating Systems (OS)
textbook_reference: Abraham Silberschatz & Peter Galvin, Operating System Concepts, Chapter 7, Page 294

recent_questions: What is a Resource Allocation Graph (RAG)? Draw the RAG representing a deadlock state (May 2024).
Explain deadlock detection using Resource Allocation Graph with a neat sketch (Dec 2023).

theory: A Resource Allocation Graph (RAG) is a directed graph used to represent the state of resource allocation in an operating system. It consists of processes and resources, and is used to detect the presence of a deadlock. Process nodes are represented as circles, and resource nodes are represented as rectangles/squares, with small dots representing resource instances. Directed edges from processes to resources represent requests, and directed edges from resource instances to processes represent allocations. A cycle in a single-instance RAG indicates a deadlock state.
key_points: Process nodes are circles; Resource nodes are rectangles/squares containing instance dots.
Request edges point from process to resource; Assignment edges point from resource instance to process.
If all resources are single-instance, any cycle in the RAG guarantees a deadlock.
If resources have multiple instances, a cycle is a necessary but not sufficient condition for deadlock.
RAG is used by deadlock detection algorithms to identify if the system is in an unsafe/deadlocked state.
use_cases: Deadlock Detection in Operating Systems: Handled by the OS scheduler to track resource claims.
Database Transaction Management: Identifying lock contention and deadlock cycles in database engines.

title: ER Diagram Notation Reference Table
subject: Database Management Systems (DBMS)
textbook_reference: Korth, Silberschatz & Sudarshan, Database System Concepts, Chapter 2 (Entity-Relationship Model)

recent_questions: List and explain the notation symbols used in ER diagram (Dec 2022).
Draw symbols for weak entity, multi-valued attribute, identifying relationship, and key attribute in ER model (May 2023).

theory: The Entity-Relationship (ER) model uses specific graphic representations to model database schemas. These symbols represent entities, attributes, and relationships. Standard notations include: (1) Rectangles for entity sets, (2) Ellipses for attributes, (3) Diamonds for relationship sets, (4) Double Rectangles for weak entity sets, (5) Double Ellipses for multi-valued attributes, (6) Dashed Ellipses for derived attributes, (7) Underlined text inside an ellipse for primary key attributes, and (8) Double Diamonds for identifying relationship sets of weak entities. Cardinaility and participation (total vs partial) constraints are also represented using directed/undirected lines and double lines.
key_points: Rectangles denote Entity Sets, which are real-world objects with distinct existence.
Diamonds denote Relationship Sets, representing associations between entity sets.
Ellipses denote Attributes, representing properties of entities.
Double shapes indicate 'Weak' or dependent components (Weak Entity, Identifying Relationship, Multi-valued Attribute).
Dashed/Dotted lines or ellipses denote derived or calculated values.
Underlining key attributes signifies the primary identifier constraint.
use_cases: Creating logical database design diagrams prior to SQL table creation.
Documenting database schemas for communication with system developers.

rows.value: Represents a principal, independent object type
Dependent on a strong identifying entity set
Represents an association between entity sets
Links a weak entity to its owner entity set
Represents a basic property of an entity
Can store multiple values (e.g., Phone Number)
Computed from other attributes (e.g., Age from DOB)

title: DBMS Transaction State Transition Diagram
subject: Database Management Systems (DBMS)
textbook_reference: Korth, Silberschatz & Sudarshan, Database System Concepts, Chapter 14 (Transactions)

recent_questions: Explain transaction states with a neat transition diagram. (Dec 2023)
Draw and explain the state transition diagram of a transaction. (May 2024)

theory: A transaction in a DBMS is a logical unit of processing that transitions through various states during its execution. These states are: (1) Active: The initial state where the transaction executes read and write operations; (2) Partially Committed: Reached after executing the final statement, where modifications are in volatile buffers but not yet on disk; (3) Committed: Reached after database updates are successfully and permanently written to disk; (4) Failed: Entered if normal execution cannot proceed due to hardware or logical errors; and (5) Aborted: Entered after rolling back changes and restoring the database to its pre-transaction state. Rollback ensures the Atomicity property of the database.
key_points: Active: Initial state during read/write execution.
Partially Committed: Execution is complete, but data remains in volatile RAM buffers.
Committed: Data is permanently written to disk, achieving durability.
Failed: Entered upon system crash or logical errors during execution.
Aborted: Entered after database rollback completes, ensuring atomicity.
use_cases: Transaction management and crash recovery (e.g. ARIES protocol).
Concurrency control to maintain isolation levels during transaction execution.

title: B+ Tree Structure — Index & Leaf Node Layout
subject: Database Management Systems (DBMS)
syllabus_reference: Module 4: Indexing and Hashing (B+ Tree Index)
textbook_reference: Korth, Silberschatz & Sudarshan, Database System Concepts, Chapter 11 (Indexing), Page 481

recent_questions: Explain the structure of a B+ tree. How is it used for indexing in databases? Explain insertion and deletion with examples (Dec 2023).
What is a B+ tree? Differentiate between a B-tree and B+ tree. Draw a B+ tree of order 3 for keys 5, 8, 10, 15, 20, 22, 25, 28, 40, 45, 55, 60 (May 2024).

theory: A B+ Tree is a self-balancing tree data structure commonly used in database systems and file systems for efficient storage and retrieval of sorted data. Unlike a B-tree, all data values (key + record pointer) are stored exclusively in the leaf nodes. The internal (index) nodes contain only separator keys to guide the search. The leaf nodes are linked in a sorted linked list, enabling efficient sequential scans and range queries. For a B+ tree of order n, internal nodes have at most n child pointers and n-1 keys; leaf nodes have at most n-1 key-pointer pairs. The tree is always height-balanced: all leaves reside at the same depth.
key_points: All actual data records are stored ONLY in leaf nodes; internal nodes hold only keys for routing.
Leaf nodes are linked in a sorted sequential linked list for efficient range queries.
Order-n B+ tree: internal nodes have at most n-1 keys and n child pointers.
Guaranteed O(log n) search, insert, and delete operations.
B+ tree is the default index structure in MySQL InnoDB, PostgreSQL, Oracle, and SQL Server.
Splits propagate upward on insert; borrows or merges occur on delete underflow.
use_cases: Primary and secondary indexing in relational database management systems.
File system directory indexing (e.g., NTFS directory index).
Efficient range-query processing in data warehouses and OLAP systems.

title: OSI Reference Model (7 Layers)
subject: Computer Networks (CN)
textbook_reference: Andrew S. Tanenbaum, Computer Networks, Prentice Hall, Chapter 1, Page 38

recent_questions: Explain the functions of each layer in the OSI reference model with a neat diagram (Dec 2023).
Compare OSI reference model and TCP/IP reference model (May 2023).

theory: The Open Systems Interconnection (OSI) reference model is an ISO-standard abstract framework describing how data from an application on one computer moves through a network medium to an application on another. It divides network communications into seven logical layers, each serving the layer above it and being served by the layer below it. The layers are: (7) Application: User interface; (6) Presentation: Data formatting and encryption; (5) Session: Dialog control; (4) Transport: End-to-end reliability; (3) Network: Routing and logical addressing; (2) Data Link: Framing and physical addressing; (1) Physical: Raw bit transmission. Data flows down from Layer 7 to Layer 1 on the sending host, and back up from Layer 1 to Layer 7 on the receiving host.
key_points: Application (L7): Interfaces user applications (HTTP, FTP, SMTP, DNS).
Presentation (L6): Handles syntax, compression, encryption, and translation (SSL, JPEG, ASCII).
Session (L5): Establishes, maintains, and synchronizes conversations (NetBIOS, RPC).
Transport (L4): Manages flow control, error recovery, and segmentation (TCP, UDP).
Network (L3): Determines optimal path routing using IP addresses (IP, ICMP, routers).
Data Link (L2): Prepares data frames for physical transfer using MAC addresses (Ethernet, switches).
Physical (L1): Transmits binary bit streams over copper, fiber, or air medium (hubs, cables).
use_cases: Standardizing network protocols and interfaces across vendors.
Troubleshooting network connectivity issues by isolating problems layer by layer.

title: TCP 3-Way Handshake Connection Establishment
subject: Computer Networks (CN)
textbook_reference: Behrouz A. Forouzan, Data Communications and Networking, Chapter 23, Page 734

recent_questions: Explain the TCP connection establishment process (3-way handshake) with a timing diagram (May 2024).
Draw and explain TCP state transition diagram for connection termination (Dec 2023).

theory: Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) is a connection-oriented transport layer protocol that guarantees reliable delivery of data packets. Before any data can be transferred, TCP establishes a virtual connection between a Client and a Server using a three-step handshake process. (1) Segment 1 (SYN): The client sends a synchronization packet with an initial sequence number (client_isn) to the server. (2) Segment 2 (SYN-ACK): The server receives the SYN, acknowledges it by setting the acknowledgment number to client_isn + 1, and sends its own sequence number (server_isn) along with the SYN flag set. (3) Segment 3 (ACK): The client receives the SYN-ACK, acknowledges it by setting the acknowledgment number to server_isn + 1, and sends an ACK packet back to the server. Both sides are now in the ESTABLISHED state.
key_points: SYN: Sent by client to synchronize sequence numbers. Client state becomes SYN_SENT.
SYN-ACK: Sent by server to acknowledge client's SYN and send its own SYN. Server state becomes SYN_RCVD.
ACK: Sent by client to acknowledge server's SYN. Both transition to ESTABLISHED.
Sequence numbers protect against duplicate, lost, or out-of-order packets.
Handshake parameters exchange maximum segment size (MSS) and window scale factor.
use_cases: Establishing reliable connections for web browsing (HTTP/HTTPS) and file transfers (FTP).
Analyzing network packet traces (Wireshark) to diagnose slow connection establishment.

title: DFD Level 0 & Level 1 — Library Management System
subject: Software Engineering (SE)
syllabus_reference: Module 3: Requirements Analysis & Specification (Data Flow Diagrams)
textbook_reference: Roger S. Pressman, Software Engineering: A Practitioner's Approach, Chapter 7, Page 185

recent_questions: Draw Level 0 (Context Diagram) and Level 1 DFD for a Library Management System (May 2024).
Explain the rules for constructing Data Flow Diagrams. Draw DFD for Hotel Management System (Dec 2023).

theory: A Data Flow Diagram (DFD) is a graphical modeling tool used to represent the flow of data through an information system. It outlines inputs, processes, data stores, and outputs. DFDs are constructed hierarchically. Level-0 (also called the Context Diagram) models the entire system as a single process bubble connected to external entities (such as students or librarians), showing overall boundaries. Level-1 DFD refactors the Level-0 process bubble into multiple sub-processes (e.g. searching books, issuing books, returning books) and introduces data stores (e.g. books database, student files) to show where data is stored within the system.
key_points: Context Diagram (Level-0) must contain exactly one process bubble representing the entire system.
External Entities (Squares) represent sinks/sources of data outside the system boundary.
Process Bubbles (Circles/Rounded Rectangles) represent operations transforming inputs to outputs.
Data Stores (Open Rectangles) represent resting files, databases, or memory structures.
Rule: Data cannot flow directly between two external entities, or directly between two data stores.
use_cases: Structured systems analysis to map data flows before database schema design.
Documenting software system requirements for developers and non-technical stakeholders.

title: TCP/IP Model — 4 Layers
subject: Computer Networks (CN)
textbook_reference: Andrew S. Tanenbaum, Computer Networks, Prentice Hall, Chapter 1, Page 44

recent_questions: Compare OSI reference model and TCP/IP reference model. (Dec 2023, May 2022)
Explain different layers of TCP/IP reference model with a diagram. (Dec 2022)

theory:
The TCP/IP Model (also called the Internet Model or DoD Model) is the practical network model developed by DARPA in the 1970s that forms the foundation of the modern Internet. Unlike the OSI model's 7 layers, TCP/IP uses 4 layers, and it defines the protocols actually implemented on computers worldwide.

Layer 4 — Application Layer: Combines the functions of OSI layers 5 (Session), 6 (Presentation), and 7 (Application). Provides application-level protocols for end-user services: HTTP/HTTPS for web browsing, FTP for file transfer, SMTP/POP3/IMAP for email, DNS for domain resolution, SSH for secure remote access.

Layer 3 — Transport Layer: Equivalent to OSI Layer 4. Provides end-to-end communication between host processes identified by port numbers. TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) provides reliable, ordered, error-checked delivery via the three-way handshake, flow control (sliding window), and congestion control. UDP (User Datagram Protocol) provides fast, connectionless, unreliable delivery for applications like DNS, video streaming, and VoIP.

Layer 2 — Internet Layer: Equivalent to OSI Layer 3. Handles logical addressing and routing. IP (Internet Protocol) assigns unique IP addresses to hosts and routes packets across networks. IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses (4.3 billion); IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses. ICMP handles error messages (used by ping, traceroute). ARP maps IP addresses to MAC addresses.

Layer 1 — Network Access Layer: Combines OSI Layers 1 (Physical) and 2 (Data Link). Handles transmission of data frames over the physical network medium — Ethernet, Wi-Fi, fiber optic cable. Handles MAC addressing, framing, and error detection at the hardware level.

Key Difference from OSI: TCP/IP is descriptive (describes existing protocols); OSI is prescriptive (a theoretical standard). TCP/IP is the model that actually runs the Internet.
key_points: TCP/IP has 4 layers: Application, Transport, Internet, Network Access (vs OSI's 7 layers)
Application layer combines OSI Session + Presentation + Application layers
TCP = reliable, connection-oriented (handshake); UDP = fast, connectionless (no handshake)
Internet layer (IP) provides logical addressing; Network Access provides physical addressing (MAC)
TCP/IP is the actual protocol suite used on the Internet; OSI is the theoretical reference model
use_cases: The entire Internet — all web browsing, email, streaming, and cloud services run on TCP/IP
Corporate LANs and WANs — TCP/IP is the universal standard for modern networking
IoT devices — embedded devices use TCP/IP (especially UDP) for lightweight communication

title: DNS Resolution Process
subject: Computer Networks (CN)
syllabus_reference: Module 6: Application Layer Protocols (Domain Name System - DNS)
textbook_reference: Behrouz A. Forouzan, Data Communications and Networking, Chapter 25, Page 782

recent_questions: Explain DNS resolution process with a neat diagram. (Dec 2023)
What are recursive and iterative queries in DNS? Explain with an example. (May 2024)

theory:
DNS (Domain Name System) is the internet's distributed hierarchical naming system that translates human-readable domain names (like www.google.com) into machine-readable IP addresses (like 142.250.80.46). Without DNS, users would have to memorize IP addresses for every website. DNS is often described as the 'phone book of the internet.'

DNS Hierarchy: The DNS namespace is organized as an inverted tree with four levels. The root zone ('.') is at the top, managed by ICANN. Below it are Top-Level Domains (TLDs) like .com, .org, .net, country-code TLDs (.uk, .in). Below TLDs are Second-Level Domains (google, amazon) and below those are subdomains (www, mail, ftp). Reading a fully qualified domain name (FQDN) right-to-left follows this hierarchy: www.example.com. = subdomain.domain.TLD.root.

Recursive vs Iterative Queries: The client OS makes a recursive query to the resolver — asking the resolver to do all the work and return a final answer. The resolver then makes iterative queries to root and TLD servers — each returns a referral (not the final answer). This distinction is key: the resolver does the 'legwork' so the client doesn't have to.

DNS Caching and TTL: Every DNS record has a TTL (Time-To-Live) value in seconds. Once a resolver caches a result, it serves that cached answer for the TTL duration without querying again. Common TTLs: 3600s (1 hour), 86400s (24 hours). Low TTL allows rapid DNS changes (useful during migrations). High TTL reduces DNS query load. Negative caching (RFC 2308) also caches 'not found' responses.

DNS Record Types: A (IPv4 address), AAAA (IPv6 address), CNAME (alias/canonical name), MX (mail exchange), NS (name server), TXT (text — used for SPF, DKIM email validation), PTR (reverse DNS lookup — IP to name), SOA (Start of Authority — zone metadata). Understanding these record types is critical for network administration and troubleshooting.

DNS Security: DNS was designed without authentication, creating vulnerabilities. DNSSEC (DNS Security Extensions) adds cryptographic signatures to DNS records. DNS over HTTPS (DoH) and DNS over TLS (DoT) encrypt DNS queries to prevent eavesdropping. DNS cache poisoning (Kaminsky attack) involves injecting forged DNS responses into a resolver's cache to redirect users to malicious sites.
key_points: DNS resolution follows a hierarchy: Browser cache → Hosts file → Recursive resolver → Root NS → TLD NS → Authoritative NS
Recursive resolver (ISP's DNS) does all the iterative queries — the client only talks to the resolver
13 root name server clusters (a-m.root-servers.net) — each is actually many anycast servers worldwide
TTL controls how long DNS responses are cached; lower TTL = faster propagation of changes but higher DNS load
DNS uses UDP port 53 for queries (faster, no connection overhead); switches to TCP port 53 for responses >512 bytes or zone transfers
Key record types: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), CNAME (alias), MX (mail), NS (nameserver), TXT (verification)
use_cases: Web browsing — every URL typed in a browser triggers DNS resolution to find the server's IP address
Email routing — DNS MX records direct email to the correct mail server for a domain
Load balancing and failover — multiple A records or low-TTL DNS enables traffic distribution and rapid failover to backup servers

title: ARP Protocol — Address Resolution Process
subject: Computer Networks (CN)
syllabus_reference: Module 3: Network Layer Protocols (Address Resolution Protocol)
textbook_reference: Behrouz A. Forouzan, Data Communications and Networking, Chapter 19, Page 588

recent_questions: Explain the working of Address Resolution Protocol (ARP) with a sequence diagram. (May 2024)
Write short note on ARP, Proxy ARP and Gratuitous ARP. (Dec 2023)

theory:
ARP (Address Resolution Protocol), defined in RFC 826 (1982), is a network communication protocol used to discover the hardware (MAC) address of a device from its known IP address within a local network. ARP operates at the boundary of Layer 2 (Data Link) and Layer 3 (Network) of the OSI model.

Why ARP is Needed: IP addresses (Layer 3) are logical addresses used for routing across networks. However, actual data frame delivery on a local network (LAN) requires Layer 2 MAC addresses. When a host knows the IP address of the destination but not its MAC address, it uses ARP to discover it. Without ARP, Ethernet frames cannot be correctly addressed for local delivery.

ARP Request — Broadcast: The requesting host constructs an ARP Request packet with its own IP and MAC as the sender fields, and the target IP address as the destination. Since the target MAC is unknown, the Ethernet frame is broadcast to FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF — every device on the LAN receives and processes this. The ARP packet contains opcode 1 (Request).

ARP Reply — Unicast: The host that owns the target IP address sends an ARP Reply directly (unicast) back to the requester. The reply contains the sender's MAC address. All other hosts simply discard the broadcast. The ARP packet contains opcode 2 (Reply).

ARP Cache: To avoid repeating ARP lookups for every packet, hosts maintain an ARP cache (ARP table) — a temporary mapping of IP addresses to MAC addresses. Dynamic entries typically expire after 20 minutes (varies by OS). The command `arp -a` displays the current ARP table. Static ARP entries can be configured manually for security.

Special ARP Types: Gratuitous ARP — a host broadcasts an ARP reply of its own IP-to-MAC mapping, used after IP address changes or NIC replacement to update neighbors' ARP caches. Proxy ARP — a router responds to ARP requests for hosts on other subnets, enabling inter-subnet communication without the host knowing.

ARP Security Issues: ARP has no authentication mechanism, making it vulnerable to ARP Spoofing (ARP Poisoning) attacks where an attacker sends forged ARP replies to associate their MAC with another host's IP, enabling Man-in-the-Middle (MITM) attacks. Defenses include Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on managed switches and static ARP entries for critical hosts.
key_points: ARP maps Layer 3 IP addresses to Layer 2 MAC addresses within a local subnet
ARP Request is BROADCAST (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) — all hosts on subnet receive it
ARP Reply is UNICAST — only the requesting host receives the reply
ARP cache stores IP→MAC mappings temporarily (default ~20 min) to reduce broadcast traffic
Gratuitous ARP: host broadcasts its own IP→MAC mapping to update neighbors' caches proactively
ARP Spoofing is a common MITM attack; Dynamic ARP Inspection (DAI) on switches prevents it
use_cases: Local network communication — any time two hosts on the same subnet communicate, ARP resolves IP to MAC
Default gateway resolution — end devices use ARP to find the MAC address of their router/gateway
Network troubleshooting — ARP table inspection (`arp -a`) helps diagnose Layer 2 connectivity issues and detect ARP spoofing attacks

title: CSMA/CD — Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection
subject: Computer Networks (CN)
syllabus_reference: Module 2: Media Access Control (CSMA/CD access method)
textbook_reference: Andrew S. Tanenbaum, Computer Networks, Prentice Hall, Chapter 4, Page 254

recent_questions: Explain CSMA/CD protocol with a neat flow chart. (May 2024)
What is binary exponential backoff algorithm in CSMA/CD? Explain. (Dec 2023)

theory:
CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection) is the Media Access Control (MAC) protocol used in traditional wired Ethernet networks (IEEE 802.3). It governs how multiple stations share a single communication channel (the shared Ethernet bus or hub-based network) without a central controller.

Three Key Concepts: (1) Carrier Sense (CS) — before transmitting, a station 'listens' to the medium to detect if it's already in use. If the channel is busy, the station defers transmission. (2) Multiple Access (MA) — multiple stations share the same communication medium (the Ethernet bus). Any station can transmit when the channel appears idle. (3) Collision Detection (CD) — while transmitting, a station simultaneously monitors the channel. If the transmitted signal differs from what's expected (voltage level increases due to signal superposition), a collision is detected immediately.

Collision and Jam Signal: A collision occurs when two stations sense an idle channel at nearly the same moment and begin transmitting simultaneously. Due to propagation delay, neither station detects the other's signal immediately. The colliding signals corrupt each other. Upon detecting a collision, all transmitting stations stop data transmission and broadcast a 32-bit jam signal to guarantee every station on the network is aware of the collision.

Binary Exponential Backoff Algorithm: After a collision, each involved station independently computes a random backoff delay using the formula: wait = random(0, 2^min(k, 10) − 1) × slot_time, where k is the collision count for this frame. The slot time for 10Mbps Ethernet is 51.2 microseconds (the time for a signal to travel the maximum network diameter and return). After the first collision (k=1), each station waits 0 or 1 slot times. After k=2, the range is 0–3 slots. After k=10+, the range is 0–1023. This exponential expansion makes repeated collisions less likely because the probability of two stations choosing the same random wait decreases as the range grows.

Maximum Retransmission Limit: If a frame has collided 16 times, the station abandons transmission and reports an error to the higher layer. This prevents a single frame from indefinitely consuming bandwidth and allows upper-layer protocols (like TCP) to handle recovery.

CSMA/CD vs CSMA/CA: CSMA/CD (collision detect) is used in wired Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) because the collision signal is detectable as a voltage anomaly on the wire. CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance) is used in WiFi (IEEE 802.11) because in wireless transmission, a station cannot transmit and receive simultaneously — it cannot detect its own collision. Wi-Fi instead uses RTS/CTS (Ready to Send/Clear to Send) and random backoff before transmission to avoid collisions.

Modern Relevance: CSMA/CD is largely obsolete in modern networks. Full-duplex switched Ethernet (using network switches instead of hubs) eliminates collisions entirely because each link is a dedicated point-to-point connection. CSMA/CD knowledge remains essential for understanding legacy networks and Ethernet fundamentals.
key_points: CSMA/CD: Carrier Sense (listen before talk) + Multiple Access (shared medium) + Collision Detection (abort on collision)
Jam signal = 32 bits — ensures all stations detect the collision despite propagation delay
Binary Exponential Backoff: after k collisions, wait random(0, 2^min(k,10)−1) × 51.2μs (for 10Mbps)
Maximum 16 retransmission attempts — after 16 failures, frame is aborted and error reported to upper layer
CSMA/CD used in IEEE 802.3 (wired Ethernet); CSMA/CA used in IEEE 802.11 (WiFi)
Full-duplex switched Ethernet eliminates collisions — CSMA/CD is only relevant in half-duplex/hub-based LANs
use_cases: Legacy Ethernet LANs using hubs — CSMA/CD managed contention on the shared half-duplex bus
Understanding IEEE 802.3 standard — CSMA/CD is the foundational MAC mechanism for all Ethernet history
Comparing wireless vs wired access methods — CSMA/CA (WiFi) vs CSMA/CD (Ethernet) is a common exam topic

title: Ethernet Frame Structure (IEEE 802.3)
subject: Computer Networks (CN)
syllabus_reference: Module 2: Data Link Layer Standards (Ethernet Frame structure)
textbook_reference: Andrew S. Tanenbaum, Computer Networks, Prentice Hall, Chapter 4, Page 272

recent_questions: Explain the frame format of IEEE 802.3 Ethernet. (Dec 2023)
What is the importance of minimum frame size in Ethernet? (May 2023)

theory:
An Ethernet frame is the Protocol Data Unit (PDU) at the Data Link Layer (Layer 2) of the OSI model. Defined by IEEE 802.3, it is the fundamental unit of data transmission on Ethernet networks. Every frame has a fixed structure with well-defined fields that enable addressing, error detection, and protocol identification.

Preamble and SFD: The 7-byte Preamble consists of alternating 1s and 0s (10101010 repeated). This pattern allows the receiving network interface card (NIC) to synchronize its clock with the incoming bit stream — a process called clock recovery. The 1-byte Start Frame Delimiter (SFD = 10101011) immediately follows and signals that the actual frame data begins in the very next byte. Note: The Preamble and SFD are added by the physical layer and are NOT counted in the official 64–1518 byte frame size.

MAC Addresses — Destination and Source: Each field is 6 bytes (48 bits) long, typically expressed in hexadecimal (e.g., AA:BB:CC:DD:EE:FF). The Destination MAC tells the switch/NIC which device should receive this frame. The Source MAC identifies the sender. Special MAC addresses: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF is the broadcast address (all devices on the LAN accept it). A MAC address with LSB of the first byte = 1 is a multicast address. LSB = 0 indicates unicast.

EtherType / Length Field: This 2-byte field has dual purpose. If the value is greater than 1500 (0x05DC), it specifies the Layer 3 protocol type encapsulated in the payload: 0x0800 = IPv4, 0x0806 = ARP, 0x86DD = IPv6, 0x8100 = VLAN tagged (IEEE 802.1Q). If the value is 1500 or less, it specifies the length of the payload in bytes (used in the original 802.3 format).

Data / Payload: Contains the encapsulated higher-layer protocol data (usually an IP packet). The minimum payload size is 46 bytes — if the data is smaller, the frame is padded with zeros to meet the minimum. This minimum ensures the frame is at least 64 bytes long, which is required for CSMA/CD collision detection to work correctly at 10Mbps Ethernet.

FCS — Frame Check Sequence: The 4-byte FCS field contains a CRC-32 (Cyclic Redundancy Check) value computed over the Destination MAC, Source MAC, EtherType/Length, and Data fields. The sender calculates the CRC and appends it. The receiver recalculates the CRC over the received data and compares it with the FCS field. If they match, the frame is assumed error-free. If they differ, the frame is silently discarded (Layer 2 does NOT request retransmission — that's TCP's job at Layer 4).

Frame Size Constraints: Minimum frame = 64 bytes (excluding preamble+SFD). This ensures that in a 10Mbps network with the maximum cable length, a transmitting station has not finished sending before a collision signal returns. Maximum frame = 1518 bytes (1500 bytes data + 18 bytes overhead: 6+6+2+4). Exceeding the maximum produces a 'giant' frame, which is discarded. Jumbo frames (up to 9000 bytes payload) are a non-standard extension supported by some Gigabit Ethernet equipment to improve throughput.

Key Difference from OSI: The IEEE 802.3 standard divides the OSI Data Link Layer into two sublayers: the Logical Link Control (LLC) sublayer and the Media Access Control (MAC) sublayer. The LLC sublayer handles multiplexing and flow control, while the MAC sublayer handles the actual frame framing and CSMA/CD protocol.
key_points: Ethernet frame structure: Preamble(7B) + SFD(1B) + Dst MAC(6B) + Src MAC(6B) + EtherType(2B) + Data(46–1500B) + FCS(4B)
Minimum frame = 64 bytes — ensures collision detection works at 10Mbps before transmission ends
Maximum frame = 1518 bytes (1500B data + 18B overhead); Preamble+SFD (8B) not counted in size limit
EtherType >1500: protocol ID (0x0800=IPv4, 0x0806=ARP, 0x86DD=IPv6); EtherType ≤1500: payload length
FCS uses CRC-32 for error detection — corrupt frames are silently dropped at Layer 2
Broadcast MAC = FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF; Multicast has first byte LSB=1; Unicast has first byte LSB=0
use_cases: LAN communication — every data exchange on Ethernet uses this frame structure at the Data Link layer
Network protocol analysis — tools like Wireshark dissect Ethernet frames to show each field for troubleshooting
Switch operation — switches read Destination MAC from frames to make forwarding decisions via CAM table lookup

title: IPv4 Header Format
subject: Computer Networks (CN)
textbook_reference: Behrouz A. Forouzan, Data Communications and Networking, Chapter 19, Page 567

recent_questions: Draw and explain the header format of IPv4. (May 2024)
Explain the functions of various fields in the IPv4 header. (Dec 2023)

theory:
The IPv4 (Internet Protocol Version 4) header is a structured format that precedes the payload of an IPv4 packet, containing essential metadata required for routing, fragmentation, delivery, and error checking across networks. In the Mumbai University Computer Networks syllabus (sem 5, Computer Engineering), the IPv4 header is a critical topic usually referenced from Behrouz A. Forouzan's 'Data Communications and Networking' textbook (Chapter 19, Network Layer).

An IPv4 header has a variable size of 20 to 60 bytes, depending on the presence of the Options field. The header consists of the following components:

1. Version (4 bits): Defines the IP version, which is 4 (0100 in binary).

2. Internet Header Length (IHL - 4 bits): Specifies the header length in 32-bit (4-byte) words. Since it is 4 bits, the value ranges from 5 (representing 20 bytes) to 15 (representing 60 bytes). If IHL is greater than 5, options are present.

3. Differentiated Services (DSCP/ECN - 8 bits): Historically known as the Type of Service (TOS) byte, it is now divided into DSCP (Differentiated Services Code Point, 6 bits) for Quality of Service (QoS) classification and ECN (Explicit Congestion Notification, 2 bits) for network congestion notification without dropping packets.

4. Total Length (16 bits): Defines the total size of the IP packet (header + payload) in bytes. The maximum size is 65,535 bytes, though the physical layer limits this (via MTU).

5. Identification (16 bits): Used to uniquely identify fragments of an original IP packet during fragmentation.

6. Flags (3 bits): Control and identify fragments. Bit 0 is reserved (must be 0). Bit 1 is Don't Fragment (DF) — if set to 1, fragmentation is disallowed. Bit 2 is More Fragments (MF) — if set to 1, it indicates more fragments follow; if 0, this is the last fragment.

7. Fragment Offset (13 bits): Indicates where in the original packet this fragment belongs, measured in units of 8-byte blocks (called scale factor of 8).

8. Time to Live (TTL - 8 bits): Prevents packets from circulating indefinitely in loops. Decremented by 1 at each router. If TTL reaches 0, the packet is discarded and an ICMP Time Exceeded message is sent back to the sender.

9. Protocol (8 bits): Identifies the upper-layer protocol that should receive the encapsulated data (e.g., 6 for TCP, 17 for UDP, 1 for ICMP, 89 for OSPF).

10. Header Checksum (16 bits): Used for error detection over the header fields only. Recalculated and modified at each router because TTL changes.

11. Source and Destination IP Addresses (32 bits each): The logical IPv4 addresses of the sender and receiver.

12. Options (0 to 40 bytes): Optional fields for network testing, debugging, routing paths (Record Route, Source Routing), and security.
key_points: The IPv4 header has a variable length: Minimum is 20 bytes (IHL = 5) and Maximum is 60 bytes (IHL = 15)
IHL (Internet Header Length) represents length in 32-bit (4-byte) words; multiply IHL by 4 to get header size in bytes
Total Length field is 16 bits, allowing a maximum packet size of 65,535 bytes (including header and data)
Flags (3 bits) are Reserved, DF (Don't Fragment), and MF (More Fragments), which control packet fragmentation
Fragment Offset (13 bits) specifies the position of a fragment in the packet, measured in 8-byte units
Time to Live (TTL) is an 8-bit counter decremented by each hop router to prevent infinite routing loops
Protocol field (8 bits) defines the encapsulated payload type: 1 = ICMP, 6 = TCP, 17 = UDP, 89 = OSPF
Header Checksum is 16 bits and is recalculated at every hop router because the TTL field changes
use_cases: Logical Routing: Routers inspect the Destination IP Address field to forward packets across networks using routing tables.
Packet Fragmentation: Routers split packets exceeding the next hop's MTU (Maximum Transmission Unit) using Identification, Flags, and Fragment Offset.
Quality of Service (QoS): Switched networks prioritize voice, video, or data traffic based on the DSCP bits.
Loop Prevention: The TTL field ensures lost packets don't circulate forever, saving network bandwidth.

title: DHCP DORA Process
subject: Computer Networks (CN)
syllabus_reference: Module 6: Application Layer Protocols (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol - DHCP)
textbook_reference: Behrouz A. Forouzan, Data Communications and Networking, McGraw Hill, Chapter 25, Page 790

recent_questions: Explain the operation of DHCP with a neat sequence diagram showing DORA process (May 2024).
How does a client obtain an IP address dynamically? Explain DORA process in DHCP (Dec 2023).

theory:
Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) is an Application Layer protocol that dynamically assigns IP addresses and other network configuration parameters (subnet mask, default gateway, DNS servers) to devices on a network. It operates on a client-server model using UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client).

The DORA process is the four-step handshake used by a DHCP client to acquire network configurations:

1. Discover (Broadcast): The client, having no IP address, broadcasts a DHCPDISCOVER message on the local network (destination IP: 255.255.255.255, source IP: 0.0.0.0, destination MAC: FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) searching for any active DHCP server.

2. Offer (Unicast/Broadcast): Any DHCP server receiving the Discover message responds with a DHCPOFFER message. This contains an available IP address from the server's pool (scope), subnet mask, lease duration, default gateway IP, and DNS server IPs.

3. Request (Broadcast): The client selects one offer (typically the first received) and broadcasts a DHCPREQUEST message. This informs the selected server of acceptance and implicitly notifies other DHCP servers to release their offered IPs back to their pools.

4. Acknowledge (Broadcast/Unicast): The selected server receives the Request and responds with a DHCPACK message, committing the IP binding and configuration parameters to the client. Upon receiving the ACK, the client configures its network interface and transitions to the BOUND state.
key_points: DHCP is based on BOOTP and works automatically to configure IP, gateway, and DNS on hosts.
Uses UDP port 67 for server and UDP port 68 for client communications.
DORA stands for: Discover (Client to Server), Offer (Server to Client), Request (Client to Server), Ack (Server to Client).
Discover and Request are always broadcast because the client does not yet have an assigned IP address.
Offer and Ack can be unicast or broadcast depending on the server configuration and client flags.
Addresses are leased rather than permanently assigned, requiring renewal before the lease expires (T1 timer at 50% lease time).
use_cases: Automatic IP assignment in enterprise LANs, home Wi-Fi networks, and public hotspots.
Simplifying network administration by centralizing IP address management (preventing IP conflicts).

title: Intel 8086 Pin Diagram (40-Pin DIP)
subject: Microprocessors
syllabus_reference: Module 3: 8086 Microprocessor Hardware Specifications (Pin Diagram and Min/Max Modes)
textbook_reference: Douglas V. Hall, Microprocessors and Interfacing, McGraw Hill, Chapter 9, Page 270

recent_questions: Draw and explain the pin diagram of 8086 microprocessor in detail (Dec 2023).
Explain minimum and maximum modes of 8086 microprocessor. Compare the signals (May 2024).
Draw and explain 8086 pin configuration showing multiplexed pins and min/max mode signals (Dec 2022).

theory: The Intel 8086 is a 40-pin Dual In-line Package (DIP) 16-bit microprocessor. Due to physical package constraints, many of its pins are multiplexed to carry different signals depending on the current bus state or the mode of operation. The most important pin-configuration aspect of the 8086 is the MN/MX̄ (Minimum/Maximum Mode) pin 33. When connected to VCC (+5V), it puts the CPU in Minimum Mode, where it behaves as a standalone controller and generates all control signals (like ALE, RD̄, WR̄, M/IŌ, DEN̄, DT/R̄) directly. When connected to GND (0V), it puts the CPU in Maximum Mode, where it is used in multi-processor configurations with co-processors (like the 8087 Numeric Data Processor). In Maximum Mode, control signals are generated by an external 8288 Bus Controller which decodes the status lines S0̄, S1̄, and S2̄ from the CPU. To save pins, the 8086 also multiplexes the 16-bit address and data bus (AD0-AD15), requiring external latching via ALE during the first T-state (T1), and multiplexes high-order address lines with status lines (A16/S3 to A19/S6, and BHĒ/S7).
key_points: 8086 is a 40-pin DIP chip requiring a +5V power supply (VCC at pin 40, GND at pins 1 & 20).
MN/MX̄ (Pin 33) is a strap pin that determines the mode: High for Minimum Mode, Low for Maximum Mode.
Pins 24 to 31 have dual functions depending on the MN/MX̄ mode selection.
Address and data buses are multiplexed (AD0-AD15) to minimize physical pin count, using ALE to latch addresses.
High-order address lines are multiplexed with status lines (A16/S3-A19/S6) and bus high enable with status (BHĒ/S7).
Maximum mode offloads control signal generation to the 8288 Bus Controller, which decodes the S0̄, S1̄, S2̄ status outputs.
use_cases: Designing standalone, cost-sensitive 8086 systems using Minimum Mode.
Designing high-performance multi-processor architectures using 8086 with 8087 Math Co-processor and 8288 Bus Controller in Maximum Mode.
Understanding bus cycles, multiplexing, and memory interfacing in undergraduate hardware courses.

title: 8085 Opcode Fetch Timing Diagram
subject: Microprocessors
syllabus_reference: Module 2: Intel 8085 Microprocessor Architecture (Instruction Cycles, Machine Cycles, and Timing Diagrams)
textbook_reference: Ramesh S. Gaonkar, Microprocessor Architecture, Programming, and Applications with the 8085, Penram International Publishing, Chapter 4

recent_questions: Draw and explain the timing diagram of Opcode Fetch machine cycle in 8085 (May 2024).
Draw the timing diagram for opcode fetch cycle and explain the role of ALE, RD̄, and WR̄ signals (Dec 2023).
Explain Opcode Fetch machine cycle with timing diagram for 8085 (May 2023).

theory:
The Opcode Fetch cycle is the first machine cycle of every instruction in the Intel 8085 microprocessor. It consists of 4 clock cycles (T1 to T4) or 6 clock cycles depending on the instruction. During this cycle, the processor fetches the 8-bit instruction opcode from a memory location pointed to by the Program Counter (PC) and loads it into the Instruction Register (IR).

Signal-by-signal breakdown of the Opcode Fetch cycle:

1. CLK (Clock): Synchronizes all internal operations. One complete cycle consists of 4 states: T1, T2, T3, and T4.

2. Higher-order Address Bus (A8-A15): The microprocessor places the higher 8-bits of the memory address (Program Counter High, PCH) on the A8-A15 lines. This address remains stable on the bus throughout T1, T2, and T3 states.

3. Multiplexed Address/Data Bus (AD0-AD7): To reduce pin count, the 8085 multiplexes the lower-order address and the data bus. During T1, the lower-order 8-bits of the address (Program Counter Low, PCL) are placed on AD0-AD7. During T2 and T3, this bus carries the opcode (data) fetched from memory. During T4, it goes to High Impedance (Hi-Z).

4. ALE (Address Latch Enable): A positive-going pulse generated by the CPU at the beginning of T1. It goes high during T1 and goes low at the start of T2. An external latch (like 74LS373) uses this falling edge to demultiplex the address and capture the lower-order address (PCL).

5. RD̄ (Read - Active Low): Goes low during T2 and stays low through T3. This signal enables the memory read buffer, placing the opcode on the AD0-AD7 bus. It goes high in T4, terminating the read operation.

6. IO/M̄ (Input-Output / Memory): Distinguishes between Memory and I/O cycles. Since opcode fetch is a memory read, IO/M̄ stays low (0) throughout the cycle.

7. S1 and S0 (Status Signals): Define the type of machine cycle. For Opcode Fetch, both S1 and S0 are high (S1 = 1, S0 = 1).
key_points: Opcode Fetch cycle typically takes 4 T-states (T1-T4) to fetch, decode, and execute the opcode.
ALE is high only during T1 and its falling edge latches the lower address bits (A0-A7).
RD̄ is active low during T2-T3, causing the memory to put the instruction opcode on the AD0-AD7 bus.
AD0-AD7 carries PCL address during T1 and Opcode data during T2-T3, and floats (Hi-Z) in T4.
A8-A15 carries PCH address during T1-T3 and is unspecified during T4.
IO/M̄ is low (0) for memory access, and status signals S1=1, S0=1 specify an Opcode Fetch cycle.
use_cases: Understanding machine cycle execution and instruction execution flow in microprocessors.
Designing memory interfacing circuits using 8085 control signals (ALE, RD̄, WR̄).
Analyzing system bus timing using logic analyzers during embedded systems debugging.

title: Intel 8085 Pin Diagram (40-Pin DIP)
subject: Microprocessors
syllabus_reference: Module 2: Intel 8085 Microprocessor Architecture (Pin Diagram and Signal Description)
textbook_reference: Ramesh S. Gaonkar, Microprocessor Architecture, Programming, and Applications with the 8085, Penram International Publishing, Chapter 3

recent_questions: Draw and explain the pin diagram of 8085 microprocessor in detail. (May 2024)
Classify the signals of 8085 microprocessor into functional groups and explain them with a neat pinout diagram. (Dec 2023)
Draw the pin configuration of 8085 and write short notes on ALE, READY, and HOLD signals. (May 2023)

theory: The Intel 8085 is a 40-pin Dual In-line Package (DIP) 8-bit microprocessor. Due to physical package constraints, its 40 pins are divided into seven functional groups to handle address, data, control, status, power, interrupts, and DMA request operations. The 16-bit address bus is split: the upper 8-bits A8-A15 are dedicated output pins, whereas the lower 8-bits AD0-AD7 are multiplexed address/data lines that carry address during the T1 state (synchronized by a high ALE pulse) and bidirectional data during the T2 and T3 states. Control signals include RD̄ (Read), WR̄ (Write), and ALE (Address Latch Enable), while status signals S0, S1, and IO/M̄ define the current machine cycle. Five hardware interrupts (TRAP, RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, INTR) provide external prioritization, and DMA is facilitated via HOLD and HLDA lines. Power is supplied via VCC (+5V at pin 40) and VSS (GND at pin 20), with X1 and X2 (pins 1, 2) connecting the external crystal oscillator to set the system clock frequency.
key_points: 40-pin Dual In-line Package (DIP) operating at a standard +5V DC power supply.
Multiplexed AD0-AD7 (pins 12-19) carries address on ALE=1 (T1) and data on ALE=0 (T2-T3).
Dedicated A8-A15 (pins 21-28) lines carry the upper 8 bits of the memory address.
Status signals S1, S0, and IO/M̄ define the type of bus transfer operation in progress.
Supports five hardware interrupts: TRAP (non-maskable, highest priority), RST 7.5, RST 6.5, RST 5.5, and INTR (maskable, lowest).
Uses HOLD and HLDA signals for Direct Memory Access (DMA) transactions.
use_cases: Drawing a complete pin layout diagram in undergraduate examinations for 10-mark questions.
Interfacing external memory devices (RAM, EPROM) and I/O devices to 8085 using address demultiplexing.
Understanding bus control timing, signal synchronization, and DMA controller interfacing.

title: 8085 Memory Write Timing Diagram
subject: Microprocessors
syllabus_reference: Module 2: Intel 8085 Microprocessor Architecture (Instruction Cycles, Machine Cycles, and Timing Diagrams)
textbook_reference: Ramesh S. Gaonkar, Microprocessor Architecture, Programming, and Applications with the 8085, Penram International Publishing, Chapter 4

recent_questions: Draw and explain the timing diagram of Memory Write machine cycle in 8085 (Dec 2023).
Draw the timing diagram for memory write cycle and explain the role of WR̄, ALE and status signals. (May 2024).
Draw the bus timing diagram of the memory write machine cycle and write the operations performed in each T-state. (May 2023)

theory: The Memory Write machine cycle is executed by the Intel 8085 microprocessor to write 8-bit data into a specified memory location. This cycle consists of 3 clock cycles/states: T1, T2, and T3. During T1, the CPU places the higher 8-bits of the memory address (A8-A15) on the upper address bus, and the lower 8-bits of the address (A0-A7) on the multiplexed AD0-AD7 bus. ALE is pulsed high to enable latching of the lower address. In T2, the CPU places the data byte on the AD0-AD7 bus and asserts the WR̄ (Write) signal low. The memory device latches this data on the rising edge of the WR̄ signal, which occurs in the middle of the T3 state. IO/M̄ is held low (0) throughout to indicate a memory operation, and status lines are set to S1=0, S0=1 to signify a write transfer.
key_points: The Memory Write cycle of the Intel 8085 microprocessor occupies 3 T-states (T1, T2, T3).
In T1, ALE (Address Latch Enable) is high, and the multiplexed AD0-AD7 bus carries the lower-order address (A0-A7).
In T2, the CPU places write data (D0-D7) on the AD0-AD7 bus, and asserts the WR̄ (Write) control signal low.
The WR̄ signal goes high during T3, executing the write operation on its rising edge.
A8-A15 carries the higher-order address (A8-A15) and remains stable throughout T1, T2, and T3.
IO/M̄ stays low (0) for memory access, and status signals S1=0, S0=1 define a Write machine cycle.
use_cases: Analyzing the bus activity of the 8085 microprocessor during write instructions like MOV M, A or SHLD.
Interfacing SRAM/DRAM write-enable inputs with the 8085 WR̄ control signal.
Designing and testing memory write cycles using timing diagrams in academic exams and logic analyzers.

title: 8085 Memory Read Timing Diagram
subject: Microprocessors
syllabus_reference: Module 2: Intel 8085 Microprocessor Architecture (Instruction Cycles, Machine Cycles, and Timing Diagrams)
textbook_reference: Ramesh S. Gaonkar, Microprocessor Architecture, Programming, and Applications with the 8085, Penram International Publishing, Chapter 4

recent_questions: Draw and explain the timing diagram of Memory Read machine cycle in 8085 (May 2024).
Draw the timing diagram for memory read cycle and explain the role of RD̄, ALE and status signals. (Dec 2023).
Draw the bus timing diagram of the memory read machine cycle and write the operations performed in each T-state. (May 2023)

theory: The Memory Read machine cycle is executed by the Intel 8085 microprocessor to read 8-bit data from a specified memory location. This cycle consists of 3 clock cycles/states: T1, T2, and T3. During T1, the CPU places the higher 8-bits of the memory address (A8-A15) on the upper address bus, and the lower 8-bits of the address (A0-A7) on the multiplexed AD0-AD7 bus. ALE is pulsed high to enable latching of the lower address. In T2, the lower address is removed, the bus floats, and the CPU asserts the RD̄ (Read) signal low. The memory device places the data byte on the AD0-AD7 bus. In T3, the CPU reads the data on the rising edge of the CLK, and pulls the RD̄ signal high. This disables the memory chip's output buffer and floats the bus. IO/M̄ is held low (0) throughout to indicate a memory operation, and status lines are set to S1=1, S0=0 to signify a read transfer.
key_points: The Memory Read cycle of the Intel 8085 microprocessor occupies 3 T-states (T1, T2, T3).
In T1, ALE (Address Latch Enable) is high, and the multiplexed AD0-AD7 bus carries the lower-order address (A0-A7).
In T2, the CPU asserts the RD̄ (Read) control signal low, enabling the selected memory device to output data.
The RD̄ signal goes high during T3, and the CPU reads the data on the rising edge of the CLK.
A8-A15 carries the higher-order address (A8-A15) and remains stable throughout T1, T2, and T3.
IO/M̄ stays low (0) for memory access, and status signals S1=1, S0=0 define a Read machine cycle.
use_cases: Analyzing the bus activity of the 8085 microprocessor during read instructions like MOV A, M or LDA.
Interfacing SRAM/EPROM read-enable inputs with the 8085 RD̄ control signal.
Designing and testing memory read cycles using timing diagrams in academic exams and logic analyzers.

title: Intel 8051 Microcontroller Pin Configuration (40-Pin DIP)
subject: Microprocessors (MP)
textbook_reference: Muhammad Ali Mazidi, The 8051 Microcontroller and Embedded Systems, Pearson, Chapter 1, Page 18

recent_questions: Draw and explain the pin diagram of 8051 microcontroller (Dec 2023).
Describe the functions of Port 0, Port 3, EA, ALE, and PSEN pins of the 8051 (May 2024).

theory: The Intel 8051 is a complete single-chip 8-bit microcontroller with 4KB ROM, 128B RAM, 4 I/O ports, 2 timers, and a full-duplex UART in a 40-pin DIP package. Port 0 (pins 32-39) is a multiplexed address/data bus; Port 3 (pins 10-17) has dual-function pins for serial I/O, external interrupts, and memory control signals.
key_points: 40-pin DIP package with Harvard architecture.
Port 0 is open-drain multiplexed AD0-AD7 (requires external pull-ups).
Port 3 provides alternate functions: RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD.
EA pin: HIGH for internal ROM, LOW for external ROM execution.
ALE pulses once per machine cycle; PSEN pulses twice during external code fetch.
XTAL1 and XTAL2 connect to a crystal oscillator for the on-chip clock.
use_cases: Embedded system design and control applications.
Industrial automation and robotics.
Educational platform for learning microcontroller architecture.

title: Signal Flow Graph (SFG) — Mason's Gain Formula
subject: Control Systems (CS)
syllabus_reference: Module 3: Signal Flow Graph and Mason's Gain Formula
textbook_reference: Ogata, Modern Control Engineering, Pearson, Chapter 2, Page 76

recent_questions: Find the transfer function using Mason's Gain Formula for the given signal flow graph (Dec 2023).
Construct the signal flow graph for a given block diagram and find the overall transfer function (May 2024).

theory: A Signal Flow Graph (SFG) is a graphical representation of a set of simultaneous linear algebraic equations describing a system. Nodes represent variables/signals, and directed branches (with transmittances) represent dependencies. Mason's Gain Formula gives the overall transfer function T = Σ(Mk·Δk)/Δ, where Mk is the gain of the k-th forward path, Δ is the graph determinant, and Δk is the co-factor of the k-th path.
key_points: Nodes represent variables; directed branches carry transmittances (gains).
Forward path: directed path from source to sink with no repeated nodes.
Loop gain: product of transmittances around a closed loop.
Δ (graph determinant) = 1 - Σ(loop gains) + Σ(product of non-touching loop gains) - ...
Δk = value of Δ with all loops touching the k-th forward path removed.
Equivalent in power to block diagram algebra but often simpler for complex systems.
use_cases: Computing overall transfer functions for complex multi-loop control systems.
Analyzing feedback amplifier circuits in electronics.
Modeling state equations for dynamic systems in modern control theory.

title: 8-Point FFT — Decimation-In-Frequency (DIF) Butterfly Diagram
subject: Digital Signal Processing (DSP)
textbook_reference: Proakis & Manolakis, Digital Signal Processing, Pearson, Chapter 8, Page 486

recent_questions: Draw the butterfly diagram for 8-point DIF-FFT. Compare DIF-FFT with DIT-FFT (Dec 2023).
Explain the Decimation-In-Frequency FFT algorithm with the signal flow graph for N=8 (May 2024).

theory: DIF-FFT (Decimation-In-Frequency) computes the N-point DFT with O((N/2)log₂N) complex multiplications instead of O(N²). The input is in natural order; the output is in bit-reversed order. Each DIF butterfly: top = A+B, bottom = (A-B)×W_N^k — twiddle factor applied AFTER the butterfly operation. This is opposite to DIT-FFT where twiddle is applied before.
key_points: Input: natural order; Output: bit-reversed order (opposite of DIT).
DIF butterfly: top = A+B, bottom = (A-B)×W_N^k (twiddle AFTER subtraction).
log₂(N) stages, N/2 butterflies per stage.
Total complex multiplications: (N/2)log₂(N) — e.g., 12 for N=8 vs 64 for direct DFT.
Twiddle factor: W_N^k = e^(-j2πk/N).
use_cases: Digital signal processing — spectrum analysis and digital filtering.
OFDM modulation/demodulation in 4G/5G wireless communications.
Audio and image compression algorithms (FFT-based).

title: Intel 8255A Programmable Peripheral Interface — 40-Pin DIP
subject: Microprocessors (MP)
textbook_reference: Muhammad Ali Mazidi, The 8051 Microcontroller and Embedded Systems, Pearson, Chapter 14; Gaonkar, Microprocessor Architecture, Pearson, Chapter 14, Page 412

recent_questions: Draw and explain the pin diagram of 8255A PPI (Dec 2023).
Describe the function of each pin group of 8255A. Explain how ports are selected using A0 and A1 (May 2024).

theory: The Intel 8255A is a 40-pin DIP Programmable Peripheral Interface with 3 ports (A, B, C) providing 24 I/O lines. CS̄, RD̄, WR̄ control data transfers; A0/A1 select the port or control register.
key_points: Port A (PA0-PA7): Pins 1-4 and 37-40, 8-bit bidirectional, Modes 0/1/2.
Port B (PB0-PB7): Pins 18-20 and 21-25, Modes 0/1.
Port C (PC0-PC7): Pins 14-17 (lower) and 10-13 (upper), used as I/O or handshake signals.
D0-D7 (Pins 27-34): Bidirectional data bus to system.
CS̄ (Pin 6) must be LOW for any operation; RESET (Pin 35, active HIGH) forces all ports to input.
use_cases: Interfacing keyboards, LED displays, and printers to 8085/8086 systems.
ADC/DAC interfacing for embedded data acquisition.
Handshake I/O control using 8255A Mode 1 strobed I/O.

title: 8085 I/O Read Timing Diagram (IN Instruction)
subject: Microprocessors (MP)
syllabus_reference: Module 2: 8085 Timing Diagrams (I/O Read Machine Cycle)
textbook_reference: R.S. Gaonkar, Microprocessor Architecture, Programming and Applications, Pearson, Chapter 4, Page 102

recent_questions: Draw and explain the I/O Read timing diagram of the 8085 microprocessor (Dec 2023).
Differentiate between Memory Read and I/O Read machine cycles of the 8085 (May 2024).

theory: The I/O Read cycle (IN instruction) has 3 T-states. IO/M̄=1 distinguishes it from memory read. The 8-bit port address is duplicated on both A8-A15 and AD0-AD7 in T1. RD̄ goes LOW in T2; CPU reads data from peripheral in T3. Status S1=1, S0=1.
key_points: IO/M̄ = 1 (HIGH) throughout — key difference from Memory Read (IO/M̄=0).
8-bit I/O port address duplicated on both A8-A15 and AD0-AD7 in T1.
Status: S1=1, S0=1 for I/O Read; S1=1, S0=0 for Memory Read.
3 T-states (T1, T2, T3) — same duration as Memory Read cycle.
use_cases: Reading data from a peripheral (e.g., 8255 Port A, ADC) via the IN instruction.
Analyzing timing constraints when interfacing I/O devices to the 8085.
Understanding I/O vs memory-mapped I/O distinction.

title: 8085 I/O Write Timing Diagram (OUT Instruction)
subject: Microprocessors (MP)
syllabus_reference: Module 2: 8085 Timing Diagrams (I/O Write Machine Cycle)
textbook_reference: R.S. Gaonkar, Microprocessor Architecture, Programming and Applications, Pearson, Chapter 4, Page 106

recent_questions: Draw and explain the I/O Write timing diagram of the 8085 microprocessor (Dec 2023).
Compare Memory Write and I/O Write machine cycles of 8085 with timing diagrams (May 2024).
